item.title: 【OpenTelemetry support for .NET 10: A behind-the-scenes look】OpenTelemetry 对 .NET 10 的支持：幕后花絮
item.description:
【

At Grafana Labs, we are fully committed to the open source OpenTelemetry project and are actively engaged with the OTel community. Many Grafanistas spend a large proportion of their time contributing directly to OpenTelemetry upstream projects, helping make observability more powerful, reliable, and accessible for everyone as part of our big tent philosophy.

We specifically contribute to many of the OpenTelemetry SDKs, including those for Java, JavaScript, and .NET, that developers use to enrich their applications with deep insights. In my role as a software engineer here at Grafana Labs, I focus on the OpenTelemetry .NET SDK.

In June 2025, we started to work with the upstream OpenTelemetry .NET instrumentation repositories to validate and deliver native support for .NET 10, the most productive and performant version of .NET yet. And in November 2025, we were able to deliver native OpenTelemetry support for .NET 10, which was an exciting milestone for my team and the OTel community at large.

In this post, I’ll offer a behind-the-scenes look at this journey, and share a few updates about how we’re helping those who use OpenTelemetry signals within their .NET applications move to .NET 10.

An overview of .NET release cycles

Since the release of .NET 5 in 2020, Microsoft has released a new major version of .NET every November. These alternate between an odd-numbered Short Term Support (STS) release that has two years of support, and an even-numbered Long Term Support (LTS) release that is supported for three years.

Below is a graphic from the .NET site that illustrates how this works in practice:

This means .NET 10 is now the latest release of .NET, superseding both .NET 8 as LTS and .NET 9 as the latest version for those running the modern cross-platform variant of .NET. 

The benefits of .NET preview validation

The .NET team provides preview builds of the next major version of .NET, which are available for testing and validation months before the final stable version is scheduled to be released. Testing existing code bases with these candidates allows the wider .NET community to provide valuable feedback to the .NET engineering teams on new features, as well as report regressions that might have occurred during the process.

A .NET upgrade often provides performance improvements in your applications. For example, just by upgrading your .NET SDK and your Target Framework Moniker (TFM) to the latest version, you can benefit from the performance improvements that have been made in the runtime and libraries since the previous release. You don’t need to implement new features or APIs to get the performance improvements; you can just leave your code as it is.

For teams trying to take advantage of these ongoing improvements, the last thing they want is to have an upgrade blocked by a third-party dependency that introduces an incompatibility. This is what motivated us to start validating the .NET OpenTelemetry libraries with .NET 10 early in the release cycle. We wanted to ensure any blockers that may have been discovered were resolved in plenty of time.

For example, one benefit of this prerelease testing was an issue we identified in the .NET logging source generator that affected projects targeting .NET Framework using the .NET 10 SDK. By identifying the issue while .NET 10 was still in preview, we were able to resolve the regression in time for Release Candidate 2 ahead of the stable release.

OpenTelemetry support for .NET 10 

Thanks to this extended testing period, the OpenTelemetry instrumentation libraries were able to deliver native support for .NET 10, and leverage new features, the same week as .NET 10’s first stable release in November 2025. The majority of the required changes were prepared and validated in advance of the release date.

This allowed us to stabilize OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation (zero code) support for .NET 10 shortly after, as well as publish a new version of the Grafana OpenTelemetry distribution for .NET with support for .NET 10.

Benefits from this new release of .NET include:

  • Support for schema URLs for metrics and traces
  • Aligning dependencies’ versions with the major .NET version
  • New ASP.NET Core metrics for:
    • Authentication/authorization
    • Blazor
    • The memory pool

These changes also lay the groundwork for future improvements to add support for schema URLs to the OpenTelemetry console and OLTP exporters, and for resource attributes.

There is a breaking behavior change to be aware of for telemetry, where the default trace context propagator has been switched from the legacy propagator to the W3C propagator.

Another thing to watch for when using .NET 10 assemblies in.NET Framework applications is binding redirects, especially where you’ve applied them to a range such as 0.0.0.0-9.9.9.9 to avoid making regular changes to your application configuration files. As 10 is greater than 9, make sure to update them so they work for .NET 10 and beyond (maybe 99 is forward-thinking enough...?).

Wrapping up

.NET 10 introduced new levels of performance and productivity and OpenTelemetry libraries for .NET are right there alongside it, helping developers gain the deep insights they need to monitor and debug their applications.

Whether you’re using the Grafana LGTM stack (Loki for logs, Grafana for visualization, Tempo for traces, and Mimir for metrics), or another observability solution, we hope the latest version of the OpenTelemetry .NET SDK helps you unlock more value from your .NET applications. And we’re looking forward to whatever improvements OpenTelemetry and the next major version of .NET bring to observability this year.

】

在 Grafana Labs，我们完全致力于开源 OpenTelemetry 项目，并积极参与 OTel 社区。许多 Grafanista 花费大量时间直接为 OpenTelemetry 上游项目做出贡献，帮助使可观测性变得更强大、更可靠、更易于每个人使用，这是我们大帐篷理念的一部分。

我们特别为许多 OpenTelemetry SDK 做出贡献，包括 Java、JavaScript 和 .NET 的 SDK，开发人员可以使用这些 SDK 来丰富他们的应用程序并获得深刻的见解。作为 Grafana Labs 的软件工程师，我专注于 OpenTelemetry .NET SDK。

2025 年 6 月，我们开始与上游 OpenTelemetry .NET 仪器存储库合作，验证并提供对 .NET 10，迄今为止最高效、性能最高的 .NET 版本。 2025 年 11 月，我们能够为 .NET 10 提供原生 OpenTelemetry 支持，这对我的团队和整个 OTel 社区来说是一个令人兴奋的里程碑。

在这篇文章中，我将回顾这一历程的幕后花絮，并分享一些关于我们如何帮助那些在 .NET 应用程序中使用 OpenTelemetry 信号的用户迁移到 .NET 10 的更新。

概述.NET 发布周期

自 2020 年发布 .NET 5 以来，微软每年 11 月都会发布新的 .NET 主要版本。这些版本在支持两年的奇数短期支持 (STS) 版本和支持三年的偶数长期支持 (LTS) 版本之间交替。

下面是来自 .NET 站点的图形，说明了其在实践中的工作原理：

这意味着.NET 10 现在是 .NET 的最新版本，取代了 .NET 8 作为 LTS 和 .NET 9，成为运行现代跨平台 .NET 变体的最新版本。 

.NET 预览验证的好处

.NET 团队提供了 .NET 下一个主要版本的预览版本，这些版本可在最终稳定版本计划发布之前几个月进行测试和验证。使用这些候选代码测试现有代码库使更广泛的 .NET 社区能够向 .NET 工程团队提供有关新功能的宝贵反馈，并报告在此过程中可能发生的回归。

.NET 升级通常会提高应用程序的性能。例如，只需将 .NET SDK 和目标框架名字对象 (TFM) 升级到最新版本，您就可以受益于自上一版本以来运行时和库中所做的性能改进。你不需要我实施新功能或 API 以提高性能；您可以保留代码不变。

对于尝试利用这些持续改进的团队来说，他们最不想看到的就是升级被引入不兼容性的第三方依赖项阻止。这就是促使我们在发布周期早期开始使用 .NET 10 验证 .NET OpenTelemetry 库的原因。我们希望确保在充足的时间内解决可能发现的任何阻止程序。

例如，此预发布测试的一个好处是我们在 .NET 日志记录源生成器中发现的一个问题，该问题影响了针对 .NET 的项目使用 .NET 10 SDK 的框架。通过在 .NET 10 仍处于预览阶段时识别问题，我们能够在稳定版本发布之前及时解决候选版本 2 的回归问题。

OpenTelemetry 对 .NET 10 的支持

由于测试期延长，OpenTelemetry 工具库能够在 .NET 发布的同一周为 .NET 10 提供本机支持，并利用新功能10 的第一个稳定版本于 2025 年 11 月发布。大多数所需的更改都在发布日期之前准备好并进行了验证。

这使我们能够在不久后稳定对 .NET 10 的 OpenTelemetry 自动检测（零代码）支持，并发布新版本的 Grafana OpenTelemetry 发行版.NET，支持 .NET 10。

这个新版本的 .NET 的优点包括：

  • 支持指标和跟踪的架构 URL
  • 将依赖项的版本与主要 .NET 版本保持一致
  • 新的 ASP.NET Core 指标用于：
    • 身份验证/授权
    • Blazor
    • 内存池

这些更改还为未来的改进奠定了基础，以向 OpenTelemetry 控制台和 OLTP 导出器以及资源属性添加对架构 URL 的支持。

遥测有一个需要注意的重大行为更改，其中默认跟踪上下文传播器已从旧版传播器切换过来到 W3C 传播器。

在 .NET Framework 应用程序中使用 .NET 10 程序集时需要注意的另一件事是绑定重定向，特别是当您将它们应用到 0.0.0.0-9.9.9.9 等范围时，以避免定期更改应用程序配置文件。由于 10 大于 9，请务必更新它们，以便它们适用于 .NET 10 及更高版本（也许 99 就足够具有前瞻性了...？）。

总结

.NET 10 引入了新级别的性能和生产力，适用于 .NET 的 OpenTelemetry 库也随之而来，帮助开发人员获得监控所需的深入见解。

无论您使用的是 Grafana LGTM 堆栈（用于日志的 Loki、用于可视化的 Grafana、用于跟踪的 Tempo 以及用于指标的 Mimir），还是其他可观测性解决方案，我们希望最新版本的 OpenTelemetry .NET SDK 可以帮助您从 .NET 应用程序中释放更多价值。我们期待 OpenTelemetry 和 .NET 的下一个主要版本今年为可观测性带来的任何改进。

item.title: 【The evolution of OpenTelemetry: A deep dive with co-founder Ted Young】OpenTelemetry 的演变：与联合创始人 Ted Young 深入探讨
item.description:
【

Sometimes the biggest challenges in software aren’t about code — they’re about consensus. What do we call things? What do we standardize? And how do you evolve a system that thousands of companies depend on without breaking everything along the way?

In this episode of “Grafana’s Big Tent” podcast, hosts Mat Ryer, Principal Software Engineer at Grafana Labs, and Matt Toback, VP of Culture at Grafana Labs, sit down with Ted Young, co-founder of OpenTelemetry and Developer Programs Director at Grafana Labs, to discuss the evolution of observability standards, the realities of tracing adoption, and why OpenTelemetry can feel both painstakingly deliberate and relentlessly fast.

They also unpack the “three pillars” myth, the hidden complexity of logging, language-level constraints, and the push towards instrumentation that is truly zero-touch.

You can watch the full episode in the YouTube video below, or listen on Spotify or Apple Podcasts.

(Note: The following are highlights from episode 6, season 3 of “Grafana’s Big Tent” podcast. This transcript has been edited for length and clarity.)

Setting the stage: OpenTelemetry and standardization

Ted Young: OpenTelemetry is a telemetry system. You can think about it as a new way of dividing up the observability stack. We used to divide the stack up by signal. So you would take one style of observability, like tracing, logging, metrics, or profiling, and then you'd be like, “I'm going to make a logging system.” You would make a logging format, and then make a logging API and logging client to generate those logs and process them. And then you’d make a database to store those logs and a UI for looking at the logs in that database. And that would be your logging system. But then someone would come along and want a metric system. So, they would redo all of that work, but just for that metric system.

The new way of dividing it up is to say there's telemetry, there's applications and systems, and computer resources. And they're all self-describing. They're all just describing what they are doing. And when they're describing what they're doing, they are all trying to speak the same language. Because when you're trying to understand what they're doing, you're never looking at any of these pieces in isolation. You're looking at them together. So if they can all speak the same language to describe what they're doing, then it'll be a lot easier to form a comprehensive picture.

And since we're talking about describing what computers are doing — mostly what computers are doing are standardizing, like talking over network protocols. It stands to reason that it should be possible to standardize the language that systems use to describe themselves. If you wanted to do that, you would want to decouple it from things like analysis and storage and user experience stuff. All of that is greenfield.

And so that's kind of the new way of slicing up observability: saying we have telemetry, which we want to standardize, and we all want to share. 

Tracing, logs, and the myth of the “three pillars”

Ted: Traditionally, logging and distributed tracing get talked about like they're two totally separate things, but that's really kind of a human historical accident... We're discovering that tracing is just logging with the context that you always wanted for your logs. Like, "What transaction are these logs a part of”? It's crazy to me how many decades went by without us being able to just look up the other logs in the same transaction.

Mat Ryer: So you’re like that guy at the party who, you know, comes in with the more existential questions like, “What if just logs and events were really traces”? Everyone's like, “Go home.”

Ted: It's kind of the opposite. I hate being existential about this stuff. I feel like I struggle uphill against ideology because people have already pre-bucketed what these tools are useful for… Tracing is like a latency analysis tool. And it can be really expensive, which means you have to do a lot of sampling. And because you have to do a lot of sampling with tracing up front, sampling's really not useful for, like, firefighting or root cause analysis because you just don't have the data, unlike in your logging system, where you keep everything.

The only reason tracing is heavily sampled is because people were trying to add it on top of a really expensive logging system that stored everything, but had no trace context. But they couldn't touch that system and add trace context to it, so it’s almost like they built a second logging system called tracing. And since that thing had only a tiny amount of resources left over from the logging system, it was heavily sampled to do some latency analysis. So that's just a historical accident that we built it that way. That's not because tracing and logging are really different from each other.

High latency, high throughput

Ted: OpenTelemetry is this curious creature. I consider OpenTelemetry's unofficial mascot to be the racing snail from the Never-Ending Story, if you remember that thing. OpenTelemetry is high latency, but high throughput, which means when you go and engage with it on any one particular thing, you're like, “This is so slow, designed by committee.” It can really feel that way, because unlike a lot of other open source projects, OpenTelemetry doesn't really get any take-backs. Generally speaking, if we put it out there and it gets adopted and we break it, people hate us forever. Much more strongly than most things, where if it's new and we break it, we're just like, “Well, it's like new open source.”

Mat: Doesn't everyone feel like that? If someone loves it and you take it away?

Ted: No, no, no. I feel more so than other projects, OpenTelemetry gets punished hard for breaking backwards compatibility. At certain levels or layers, we have to be very strict about it. With the API or data layer, in particular, if we create any kind of dependency conflict where, say, this library depends on OpenTelemetry 1.0 and this other library depends on OpenTelemetry 2.0, now these libraries won't compose because of OpenTelemetry. So we really have to care about it.

Why didn’t tracing take off sooner?

Matt Toback: Do you think that if the fundamental architecture was different, distributed tracing would have gotten traction sooner?

Ted: I think the hardest thing with distributed tracing is installing it because the context that you're getting from it is the execution flow that you're following. But language runtimes don't really give you a way to follow that execution flow effectively from an observability standpoint. If you just rely on thread-locals in languages that have threads, you might get some of it. But work often switches railroad tracks, right? Like, work will move from one thread to another, or a set of threads will get organized into some kind of scatter-gather thing or something like that. 

The trickiest part of OpenTelemetry in every language is this context propagation mechanism that we have to come up with. And then every single piece of instrumentation has to use the same cross-cutting mechanism. That's harder to roll out and get value out of than logging or metrics, where I can just roll out metrics in this corner just for me.

If we're saying the value of tracing is this distributed context, that means you have to roll tracing out across all of those services before you get that value. So that was kind of the problem. When you combine the amount of work with people thinking, “I'm going to have to rip all this out and replace it if I ever switch vendors,” it's just kind of like dead on arrival outside of an organization like Google or Microsoft or Xerox Park where there's just this enormous internal engineering culture that can make it worthwhile to go ahead and do that. So that was the real blocker.

Moving towards zero-touch observability

Ted: The future I would like to see is that no one has to install any OpenTelemetry instrumentation anywhere because when people write software, they're thinking about how their software is going to be run. And they're instrumenting it and they're shipping a playbook for the people running their software, letting them know how to actually tune the configuration parameters that they gave them and all of that. So that's the world I want to see. A lot of people right now don't think about observability and I think, in part, it’s because they don't have tools to instrument their own stuff. Without OpenTelemetry, it's actually hard to do native instrumentation. So long term, I would like to see that and I would like to see everyone become more of an observability expert as a side effect of that.

Matt: Do you think AI is going to help us get there?

Ted: I feel like with AI, it's a 90% chance nothing changes except our tools are more complicated with a 10% chance that the world ends up in a Terminator disaster and like very little in the middle. I'm very bimodal in my AI predictions.

“Grafana’s Big Tent” podcast wants to hear from you. If you have a great story to share, want to join the conversation, or have any feedback, please contact the Big Tent team at bigtent@grafana.com.

】

有时，软件中最大的挑战不是代码，而是共识。我们称之为什么东西？我们标准化什么？如何在不破坏一切的情况下发展一个成千上万家公司所依赖的系统？

在本期“Grafana’s Big Tent”播客中，主持人 Grafana Labs 首席软件工程师 Mat Ryer 和 Grafana Labs 文化副总裁 Matt Toback 与 OpenTelemetry 联合创始人兼 Grafana Labs 开发者项目总监 Ted Young 坐下来讨论可观测性标准的演变、现实情况追踪采用情况，以及为什么 OpenTelemetry 让人感觉既经过深思熟虑又快速快速。

他们还揭开了“三大支柱”的神话、日志记录的隐藏复杂性、语言级限制以及真正零接触仪器的推动。

您可以在下面的 YouTube 视频中观看完整剧集，或在 Spotify 或 Apple 播客。

（注：以下是第 6 集的精彩片段， “Grafana’s Big Tent”播客的第 3 季。为了长度和清晰度，本文本经过编辑。）

准备阶段：OpenTelemetry 和标准化

Ted Young：OpenTelemetry 是一个遥测系统。您可以将其视为划分可观察性堆栈的新方法。我们过去常常按信号划分堆栈。因此，您会采用一种可观察性方式，例如跟踪、日志记录、指标或分析，然后您会说，“我要制作一个日志系统。”您将创建一个日志记录格式，然后创建一个日志记录 API 和日志记录客户端来生成这些日志并处理它们。然后，您将创建一个数据库来存储这些日志，并创建一个用于查看该数据库中的日志的 UI。那就是你的日志系统。但随后有人会出现并想要一个公制系统。因此，他们会重做所有这些工作，但只是针对该公制系统。

新的划分方式是说有遥测、有应用程序和系统以及计算机资源。它们都是自我描述的。他们都只是描述他们正在做的事情。当他们描述自己正在做的事情时，他们都试图说同一种语言。因为当你试图了解他们在做什么时，你永远不会孤立地看待这些作品中的任何一个。你正在一起看着他们。因此，如果他们都能用同一种语言来描述他们正在做的事情，那么形成一个全面的图片就会容易得多。

既然我们谈论的是描述计算机正在做什么——大多数计算机正在做的事情都是标准化的，就像通过网络协议进行交谈一样。按理说应该是可以的标准化系统用来描述自身的语言。如果您想这样做，您可能希望将其与分析、存储和用户体验等内容分离。所有这些都是未开发的领域。

这就是一种分割可观测性的新方式：我们拥有遥测技术，我们希望将其标准化，并且我们都希望共享。 

跟踪、日志和“三大支柱”的神话

Ted：传统上，日志记录和分布式跟踪被视为两个完全独立的事物，但这确实是人类历史的意外...... 我们发现跟踪只是使用您一直想要的日志上下文进行日志记录。比如，“这些日志属于哪个事务”？对我来说，几十年过去了，我们却无法在同一事务中查找其他日志，这真是太疯狂了。

Mat Ryer：所以你就像聚会上的那个人，你知道，他提出了一些更存在的问题，比如“如果日志和事件真的是痕迹怎么办？”？每个人都说，“回家吧。”

Ted：情况恰恰相反。我讨厌对这些事情存在主义。我觉得我正在与意识形态作斗争，因为人们已经预先列出了这些工具的用途……跟踪就像一个延迟分析工具。而且它可能非常昂贵，这意味着您必须进行大量采样。而且因为您必须预先进行大量的跟踪采样，所以采样对于消防或根本原因分析等确实没有用，因为您没有数据，这与您保存所有内容的日志系统不同。

跟踪被大量采样的唯一原因是人们试图将其添加到一个非常昂贵的日志系统之上，该系统存储了所有内容，但没有跟踪上下文。但他们无法接触该系统并向其添加跟踪上下文，因此几乎就像他们构建了第二个称为跟踪的日志系统。由于日志系统仅剩下极少量的资源，因此对其进行了大量采样以进行一些延迟分析。所以我们这样建造它只是一个历史意外。这并不是因为跟踪和日志记录确实彼此不同。

高延迟、高吞吐量

Ted： OpenTelemetry 是一个好奇的生物。我认为 OpenTelemetry 的非官方吉祥物是永无止境的故事中的赛跑蜗牛，如果你还记得那件事的话。 OpenTelemetry 延迟高，但吞吐量高，这意味着当您在任何一件特定的事情上使用它时，您会想，“这太慢了，是由委员会设计的。”确实有这种感觉，因为与许多其他开源项目不同，OpenTelemetry 并没有真正得到任何回报。一般来说，如果我们把它放在那里并被采用，而我们破坏了它，人们就会讨厌我们版本。比大多数事情都强烈得多，如果它是新的并且我们打破了它，我们就像，“好吧，它就像新的开源。”

Mat：不是每个人都有这样的感觉吗？如果有人喜欢它，你就把它拿走？

Ted：不，不，不。我觉得与其他项目相比，OpenTelemetry 因为破坏向后兼容性而受到严厉惩罚。在某些层面或层次上，我们必须非常严格。特别是对于 API 或数据层，如果我们创建任何类型的依赖冲突，例如，该库依赖于 OpenTelemetry 1.0，而另一个库依赖于 OpenTelemetry 2.0，那么现在这些库将不会因为 OpenTelemetry 而组合。所以我们真的必须关心它。

为什么跟踪没有早点起飞？

Matt Toback：您认为如果基本架构不同，分布式跟踪会更快获得关注吗？

Ted：我认为分布式跟踪最困难的事情是安装它，因为您从中获得的上下文是您所遵循的执行流。但是，从可观察性的角度来看，语言运行时并没有真正为您提供一种有效遵循执行流程的方法。如果您只依赖具有线程的语言中的线程局部变量，您可能会得到一些。但工作经常会改变铁轨，对吗？例如，工作将从一个线程转移到另一个线程，或者一组线程将被组织成某种分散-聚集的东西或类似的东西。 

每种语言中 OpenTelemetry 最棘手的部分是我们必须提出的上下文传播机制。然后每个仪器都必须使用相同的横切机制。这比日志记录或指标更难推出并从中获取价值，我可以在这个角落专门为我推出指标。

如果我们说跟踪的价值在于这种分布式上下文，那就意味着您必须在获得该价值之前在所有这些服务中推出跟踪。这就是问题所在。当你将工作量与人们的想法结合起来时，“如果我更换供应商，我将不得不把所有这些都拆掉并替换它”，这有点像在谷歌、微软或施乐公园这样的组织之外，那里有巨大的内部工程文化，可以让继续这样做是值得的。所以这才是真正的阻碍。

走向零接触可观察性

Ted：我希望看到的未来是没有人必须在任何地方安装任何 OpenTelemetry 仪器，因为当人们编写软件时，他们会考虑他们的软件将如何运行。他们正在对其进行检测，并为运行其软件的人员提供了一本剧本，让他们知道如何实际调整他们提供给他们的配置参数，所有这些。所以这就是我想看到的世界。现在很多人没有考虑可观察性，我认为部分原因是他们没有工具来检测自己的东西。如果没有 OpenTelemetry，实际上很难进行本机检测。从长远来看，我希望看到这一点，而且我希望看到每个人都成为可观察性专家，作为其副作用。

Matt：你认为人工智能会帮助我们实现这一目标吗？

Ted：我觉得人工智能有 90% 的可能性不会发生任何变化，只是我们的工具更加复杂，世界有 10% 的可能性最终陷入终结者灾难等中间很少。我的人工智能预测非常具有双峰性。

“Grafana 的大帐篷”播客希望听到您的声音。如果您有精彩的故事要分享、想要加入对话或有任何反馈，请通过 bigtent@grafana.com 联系 Big Tent 团队。

item.title: 【GrafanaCON 2026: Register today for our biggest community event of the year!】GrafanaCON 2026：立即注册参加我们今年最大的社区活动！
item.description:
【

Barcelona is known for its sunshine, stunning architecture, and this year GrafanaCON 2026, which will land on the Catalan coast 20-22 April.

Registration is officially open for GrafanaCON 2026, which means it’s time to secure your spot at our biggest community event of the year! We hope you’ll join us to explore the latest updates to Grafana and its extended OSS ecosystem, connect with fellow community members, and so much more.

Register today to take advantage of early bird pricing and get 50% off standard ticket prices.

(P.S. There are a limited number of discounted tickets available for this event and they’re bound to go fast!)

What to expect at GrafanaCON 2026

In addition to hands-on labs, technical deep dives, and community-led sessions, GrafanaCON 2026 includes a ton of opportunities to network with peers and discover the latest trends and tools in the open source observability space.

Some of the event highlights include:

  • The opening keynote: Hear Grafana Labs leaders and engineers share what’s new in the upcoming Grafana 13 release, along with other exciting OSS project updates.
  • The hallway track: Meet your Community Forum friends and LinkedIn connections in person to share ideas, a cup of coffee, and, of course, your favorite dashboards. 
  • Ask the Experts booth: Connect with project maintainers for Grafana, Loki, Tempo, Mimir, Pyroscope, k6, Alloy, Beyla, and more.
  • Community Garden Party: Cultivate connections with the OSS community at this can’t-miss attendee party. 
  • GrafanaCON Science Fair: Play and interact with projects demonstrating all the different things you can monitor with Grafana.
  • Golden Grot Awards: Join us in celebrating the open source community and the amazing Grafana dashboards they create.

Stay tuned to learn more about the full GrafanaCON 2026 agenda, which we will share in the coming weeks. 

Hands-on labs at GrafanaCON 2026

On Monday, 20 April, Grafana Labs experts will lead a number of hands-on labs, where you can learn how to build dashboards, data sources, telemetry pipelines, and more. 

Here’s a closer look. 

Morning sessions

Getting started with OpenTelemetry instrumentation: A practical playbook

Walk through the journey of instrumenting an application with OpenTelemetry and sending metrics, logs, and traces to the Grafana LGTM stack (Loki for logs, Grafana for visualization, Tempo for traces, and Mimir for metrics).

AI inception: Using AI to build a Grafana plugin that uses AI

Experience firsthand how large language models (LLMs) like Claude can simplify Grafana plugin development and enrich the user experience.

Dashboarding 101: Best practices to build and edit Grafana dashboards

Learn how to build, iterate, and customize a Grafana dashboard step by step, so you can quickly and confidently gain meaningful insights into your data. 

Afternoon sessions

Alloy in action: Build telemetry pipelines with OpenTelemetry and Prometheus

Use Alloy to receive, transform, and export telemetry from a sample application to observability backends within the Grafana LGTM stack.

Advanced dashboarding: Build and manage dynamic Grafana dashboards at scale

Use features like dynamic dashboards and Git Sync to build and manage production-grade Grafana dashboards at scale.

Shift left with reliability testing: A hands-on introduction to k6

Explore how to make reliability testing part of your overall observability strategy, using k6 to identify performance bottlenecks proactively. 

Note: Tickets for hands-on labs can only be purchased with a conference ticket, and you can choose up to one morning and one afternoon lab. Learn more and register on our GrafanaCON 2026 hands-on labs web page.

We can’t wait to see you in Barcelona this April! 

The countdown to GrafanaCON 2026 has begun! Register for the event today.

】

巴塞罗那以其阳光明媚、令人惊叹的建筑而闻名，今年的 GrafanaCON 2026 将于 4 月 20-22 日登陆加泰罗尼亚海岸。

GrafanaCON 2026 报名已正式开放，这意味着是时候了确保您参加我们今年最大的社区活动！我们希望您能与我们一起探索 Grafana 及其扩展 OSS 生态系统的最新更新，与其他社区成员联系等等。

立即注册即可享受早鸟价，并获得标准票价 50% 的折扣。

（P.S. 此优惠门票数量有限）活动，他们一定会进展得很快！）

GrafanaCON 2026 的期待

除了实践实验室、技术深入研究和社区主导的会议之外，GrafanaCON 2026 还提供大量与同行交流并发现开源可观测性领域最新趋势和工具的机会。

活动的一些亮点包括：

  • 开幕主题演讲：聆听 Grafana Labs 领导者和工程师分享即将发布的 Grafana 13 版本中的新增功能，以及其他令人兴奋的 OSS 项目更新。
  • 走廊轨道：与您的社区论坛朋友和 LinkedIn 联系人见面，分享想法、一杯咖啡，当然还有您最喜欢的仪表板。 
  • 专家咨询展位：与 Grafana、Loki、Tempo、Mimir、Pyrscope、k6、Alloy、Beyla 等项目维护人员联系。
  • 社区花园派对：在这个不容错过的与会者派对上培养与 OSS 社区的联系。 
  • GrafanaCON Science Fair：与展示您可以使用 Grafana 监控的所有不同事物的项目进行互动。
  • Golden Grot Awards：与我们一起庆祝开源社区和他们创建的令人惊叹的 Grafana 仪表板。

请继续关注，了解有关完整 GrafanaCON 2026 议程的更多信息，我们将在未来几周内分享。 

GrafanaCON 2026 上的实践实验室

4 月 20 日星期一，Grafana Labs 专家将主持一系列实践实验室，您可以在其中了解如何构建仪表板、数据源、遥测管道等。 

这是一个仔细的观察。 

上午会议

OpenTelemetry 仪器入门：实用手册

回顾使用 OpenTelemetry 检测应用程序，并将指标、日志和跟踪发送到 Grafana LGTM 堆栈（Loki 用于日志，Grafana 用于可视化）on，Tempo 表示轨迹，Mimir 表示指标）。

AI 启动：使用 AI 构建使用 AI 的 Grafana 插件

亲身体验如何像 Claude 这样的大型语言模型 (LLM) 可以简化 Grafana 插件开发并丰富用户体验。

仪表板 101：构建和编辑 Grafana 的最佳实践仪表板

了解如何逐步构建、迭代和自定义 Grafana 仪表板，以便您可以快速、自信地获得对数据有意义的见解。 

下午会议

Alloy 实践：使用 OpenTelemetry 和 Prometheus 构建遥测管道

使用 Alloy从示例应用程序接收、转换遥测数据并将其导出到 Grafana LGTM 堆栈中的可观测性后端。

高级仪表板：在以下位置构建和管理动态 Grafana 仪表板：规模

使用动态仪表板和 Git Sync 等功能大规模构建和管理生产级 Grafana 仪表板。

向左移动可靠性测试：实践介绍k6

探索如何将可靠性测试作为整体可观测性策略的一部分，使用 k6 主动识别性能瓶颈。 

注意：动手实验的门票只能与会议门票一起购买，并且您最多可以选择一个上午和一个下午的实验。了解更多信息并在我们的 GrafanaCON 2026 上注册动手实验网页。

我们迫不及待地想在今年四月在巴塞罗那见到您！ 

GrafanaCON 2026 倒计时已经开始！ 立即报名参加活动。

item.title: 【How to run checks on internal services with Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring】如何使用 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 对内部服务运行检查
item.description:
【

Many critical services run inside private networks, where traditional monitoring tools and practices can’t offer full visibility. This makes it difficult to validate service availability and performance before problems impact your users. 

Synthetic Monitoring — a Grafana Cloud solution that helps you proactively monitor the performance of your applications and services —  addresses this gap with a feature known as private probes. With private probes, you can run checks from within private environments, improving visibility into internal services, such as APIs, staging environments, or resources behind a firewall, without exposing them publicly. 

In this post, we walk through how to set up and run private probe checks using Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring. You can also follow along with the video below.

What exactly is a private probe?

Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring lets you run a variety of synthetic monitoring checks, such as browser, ping, DNS, HTTP, and TCP checks, from either:

  • Public probes, which are fully managed by Grafana Labs and distributed across more than 20 global locations
  • Private probes, which are self-hosted agents that you deploy within your own infrastructure

A private probe runs inside your network, executes the checks you configure, and sends the results back to Grafana Cloud. This gives you the same observability and alerting capabilities for private services that you already have for public-facing ones. Private probes are especially useful for monitoring services that are not publicly accessible, including:

  • Services running on private networks
  • Internal or private APIs
  • Staging or pre-production environments
  • Services behind authentication
  • Endpoints restricted by IP allowlists or firewalls

With private probes, you can validate service availability and performance from the same environment your users or internal systems depend on, without exposing those services to the public internet.

How to run a private probe

Setting up a private probe involves two main steps:

  • Step 1: Deploy the private probe agent
  • Step 2: Configure synthetic checks to use the private probe

Private probes can be deployed in several environments, including Kubernetes, Docker, and Ubuntu or Debian systems. In this walkthrough, we use Kubernetes. For additional deployment options and code samples, refer to the documentation.

Step 1: Deploy the private probe

Navigate to the Probes page

In Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring, navigate to the Probes page. This page lists all available public probes along with their reachability status.

Create a new private probe

Under Testing & synthetics, navigate to Synthetics → Probes. Select Add Private Probe and provide the following details:

  • A descriptive name, such as test-probe 
  • Location information, which is used for map display
  • Optional custom labels, which are helpful when managing multiple probes

Register the probe

After creating the probe, Grafana Cloud generates a one-time registration token and provides the server location. You will need both values in the next step when deploying the probe agent.

Prepare and deploy the Kubernetes manifests

To deploy the private probe to Kubernetes, you need three manifests:

  • A Namespace
  • A Secret containing the registration token
  • A Deployment for the private probe agent

These can be found in Grafana Cloud documentation. 

Configure the secret with your one-time registration token and server location. In production environments, use pass-through environment variables or a secure secret management solution instead of hardcoding tokens in YAML files.

Apply the manifests to your Kubernetes cluster in the following order:

  • Namespace
  • Secret
  • Deployment

Verify the deployment

After deployment, verify the following in your Kubernetes cluster:

  • The namespace exists
  • The probe agent pod is running
  • The probe has successfully registered with Grafana Cloud

Once registration is complete, the private probe appears on the Probes page in Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring.

Step 2: Configure checks with your private probe

With your private probe deployed and running, you can configure checks to monitor services on your private network.

Create a new check

Navigate to the Checks section and create a new check. For this example, we'll create a ping check.

Specify the target

Enter a target that isn't accessible from the public internet.

Select your private probe

In the execution section, you'll see all public probes plus your private probe. Select your private probe.

Configure frequency and alerts

Set how often the check should run (e.g., every 10 seconds for quick visibility). Optionally, configure alerts based on your monitoring requirements.

Save and run

Once created, the check begins running immediately. You'll see results populate with successful pings, response times, error rates, and other metrics.

Wrapping up

Private probes allow you to proactively monitor private services before issues affect end users. With Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring, you can use private probes to observe private services with deep visibility and reliability.

To learn more about Synthetic Monitoring, refer to our technical documentation. You can also explore this dashboard on Grafana Play, which features a variety of running checks.

Grafana Cloud is the easiest way to get started with Synthetic Monitoring. We have a generous free tier that includes 100K test executions per month and more. Sign up for free now!

】

许多关键服务在专用网络内运行，传统的监控工具和实践无法提供完整的可见性。这使得在问题影响用户之前验证服务可用性和性能变得困难。 

综合监控 - 一种 Grafana Cloud 解决方案，可帮助您主动监控应用程序和服务的性能 - 通过称为私有探针的功能弥补了这一差距。通过私有探针，您可以在私有环境中运行检查，从而提高对内部服务（例如 API、暂存环境或防火墙后面的资源）的可见性，而无需将其公开。 

在这篇文章中，我们将介绍如何使用 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 设置和运行私有探测检查。您还可以观看下面的视频。

私有探针到底是什么？

Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring 允许您运行各种综合监控检查，例如浏览器，ping、DNS、HTTP 和 TCP 检查，来自：

  • 公共探针，由 Grafana Labs 完全管理并分布在超过 20 个全球位置
  • 私有探针，它们是您在自己的基础架构中部署的自托管代理

A私有探针在您的网络内部运行，执行您配置的检查，并将结果发送回 Grafana Cloud。这为您提供了与面向公众的服务相同的私有服务可观察性和警报功能。私有探针对于监控不可公开访问的服务特别有用，包括：

  • 在私有网络上运行的服务
  • 内部或私有 API
  • 暂存或预生产环境
  • 身份验证背后的服务
  • 受 IP 白名单或防火墙限制的端点

使用私有探针，您可以在用户或内部系统所依赖的同一环境中验证服务可用性和性能开启，而不会将这些服务暴露给公共互联网。

如何运行私有探测

设置私有探测涉及两个主要步骤：

  • 步骤 1：部署私有探针代理
  • 步骤 2：配置综合检查以使用私有探针

私有探针可以部署在多种环境中，包括 Kubernetes、Docker 以及 Ubuntu 或 Debian 系统。在本演练中，我们使用 Kubernetes。有关其他部署选项和代码示例，请参阅文档。

第 1 步：部署专用探针

导航到“探针”页面

在 Grafana Cloud Synthetic 中监控，导航至探针页面。此页面列出了所有可用的公共探针及其可达性状态。

创建新的私有探针

在测试和合成下，导航至合成→探针。选择添加专用探针并提供以下详细信息：

  • 描述性名称，例如 test-probe 
  • 位置信息，用于地图显示
  • 可选的自定义标签，在管理多个探针时很有帮助

注册探针

创建探针后，Grafana Cloud 会生成一个一次性注册令牌并提供服务器位置。在下一步部署探针代理时，您将需要这两个值。

准备并部署 Kubernetes 清单

要将私有探针部署到 Kubernetes，您需要三个清单：

  • 一个 命名空间
  • 一个包含注册令牌的 Secret
  • 私有探针的部署代理

这些可以在 Grafana Cloud 文档中找到。 

使用您的一次性注册令牌和服务器位置配置密钥。在生产环境中，使用直通环境变量或安全秘密管理解决方案，而不是在 YAML 文件中硬编码令牌。

按以下顺序将清单应用到 Kubernetes 集群：

  • 命名空间
  • 秘密
  • 部署

验证部署

部署后，在 Kubernetes 中验证以下内容cluster:

  • 命名空间存在
  • 探针代理 Pod 正在运行
  • 探针已成功注册到 Grafana Cloud

注册完成后，私有探针将显示在 Grafana Cloud 综合监控的探针页面上。

步骤 2：使用私有探针配置检查

使用私有探针部署并运行后，您可以配置检查以监控专用网络上的服务。

创建新支票

导航至支票部分并创建新支票。在此示例中，我们将创建 ping 检查。

指定目标

输入无法从公共互联网访问的目标。

选择您的私有探针

在执行部分，您将看到所有公共探针以及您的私有探针。选择您的私人探测。

配置频率和警报

设置检查运行的频率（例如，每 10 秒一次以便快速查看）。 （可选）根据您的监控要求配置警报。

保存并运行

创建后，检查会立即开始运行。您将看到结果中填充了成功的 ping、响应时间、错误率和其他指标。

结束

私有探测允许您在问题影响最终用户之前主动监控私有服务。借助 Grafana Cloud Synthetic Monitoring，您可以使用私有探针来观察私有服务，具有深度可见性和可靠性。

要了解有关综合监控的更多信息，请参阅我们的技术文档。您还可以探索Grafana Play 上的此仪表板，其中包含各种运行检查。

Grafana Cloud 是开始使用综合监控的最简单方法。我们提供慷慨的免费套餐，其中包括每月 10 万次测试执行及更多。 立即免费注册！

item.title: 【Build, buy, or open source? Understanding your options with Grafana’s AI-powered observability】构建、购买还是开源？利用 Grafana 的 AI 支持的可观察性了解您的选择
item.description:
【

Some questions in engineering never go away. Here’s one that every team eventually confronts:

Do we roll up our sleeves and build the tooling ourselves, or do we buy something built for us?

It’s a choice that has the power to speed teams up or hold them back.

With the rise of AI-powered observability, this familiar software dilemma has re-emerged with higher stakes and faster-moving technology. Leaders need to decide: 

  • Should we invest time building AI capabilities ourselves? 
  • Or should we adopt an end-to-end AI experience that’s integrated, maintained, and evolves with our telemetry stack?

At Grafana Labs, we see organizations wrestling with these decisions every day. And there’s a misconception we often hear, that the choice is binary: build vs buy. But the Grafana community has long known there’s a third path: leveraging open source as the space between building and buying.

Know your options

In fact, the reality is closer to a three-lane highway approaching a junction. Each lane offers a different speed, level of control, and maintenance commitment—and each leads to a different outcome.

Let’s break it down.

Lane 1. Build: Maximum control, maximum overhead

Some organizations decide to build AI-powered observability capabilities entirely in-house. That means:

  • Designing LLM and agent orchestration
  • Wiring natural-language prompts to query generation across telemetry signals
  • Building embedding pipelines for context
  • Managing model drift and prompt libraries
  • Creating interfaces, RBAC, and workflows
  • Supporting multi-signal correlation logic
  • Maintaining, scaling, and upgrading the system over time

If offering observability experiences powered by AI is your core differentiator—for example, if you are an observability vendor yourself—then investing at this level may make sense.

But for the vast majority of teams, building means committing to months of cross-functional work before you see meaningful results—and ongoing investment long after. Industry research has repeatedly shown that building AI systems in-house involves far more than integrating a model, often slowing time to market as teams build pipelines, operate infrastructure, and ensure system reliability, all alongside ongoing core product work. 

Lane 2. Open source: Flexible and powerful, but not a complete AI solution

Open source is where Grafana’s heritage shines. Teams can:

  • Adopt Grafana, Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, Pyroscope, Alloy, and other OSS components
  • Conform to OpenTelemetry and other open standards
  • Bring their own AI through the Grafana MCP server
  • Build automation on top of OSS primitives
  • Avoid lock-in and keep infrastructure transparent

This “middle lane” is the on-ramp many organizations use as they begin exploring AI-powered observability. Open source gives you a strong foundation without requiring you to start from scratch—proven tools for collecting, storing, and querying telemetry, built on open standards and shaped by a large, active community. This flexibility allows teams to experiment, extend, and adapt their observability workflows as their systems and requirements evolve.

But open source observability is a foundation, not a complete AI solution. Turning that foundation into a usable AI-powered experience requires systems that open source doesn't provide out of the box.

Teams must design and operate their own approaches to agent orchestration, prompt design and versioning, model selection and tuning, and ongoing evaluation of AI behavior. They are responsible for deciding how context is assembled, how responses are validated, how failures are handled, and how the system is refined over time as data, usage patterns, and expectations evolve. This added responsibility is the flip side of open source’s flexibility.

For some organizations, this tradeoff is not just acceptable—it’s required. Open source offers a level of control and transparency that can be difficult for managed services to match. Teams decide where their data lives, how it flows through their systems, and which components are allowed to access it. The constraints of highly regulated environments, air-gapped networks, or organizations with strict internal compliance requirements can make open source the only viable path.

For organizations without those constraints, that tension—between flexibility and ongoing ownership—is what leads many teams to consider a managed approach.

Lane 3. Buy: Out-of-the-box intelligence, tuned to your context

Ask any engineering team today and you’ll hear the same thing: The workload rarely shrinks, but the expectations always grow. 

When you’re stretched thin, every hour counts. In that environment, you have to protect the time and focus of your team. The work that directly supports your core competency deserves your attention—and for everything else, it makes sense to lean on solutions that lighten the load.

That’s why many organizations look to managed solutions: not because they can’t build something themselves, but because it simply isn’t the best use of their team’s time. When your engineers can offload the things that aren’t central to your business, they get to spend more time on the work that moves the needle.

And building observability tooling—especially AI-powered observability—is our core competency.

At Grafana Labs, we’ve spent more than a decade helping teams understand, explore, and operate their systems. That experience doesn’t just shape our platform; it drives our commitment to building actually useful AI that enhances how engineers work every day. Our goal is not to add more dashboards or more noise—it’s to reduce toil, streamline workflows, and help people make better decisions faster.

That’s why we built two pillars of AI assistance in Grafana Cloud:

  1. Grafana Assistant is your co-pilot across the Grafana ecosystem. It helps you navigate complexity with natural-language interactions, assists with query generation and refinement, guides you through gaps in your telemetry, and makes it easier for anyone—not just beginner users or observability experts—to get answers quickly.
  2. Assistant Investigations works alongside you like a team of specialists. It continuously pulls together relevant signals, surfaces insights, identifies likely causes, and collaborates with you throughout an issue—proactively helping you understand what’s happening without requiring you to chase every detail yourself.

Both of these capabilities are built with deep intention: to make operating complex systems easier, faster, and far less burdensome. Behind the scenes, we’re putting a lot of work into making Grafana Assistant and Assistant Investigations genuinely useful for engineering teams. AI in observability isn’t something you build once—it’s something you refine constantly. 

We’ve built multiple foundational layers that shape how these AI features learn, adapt, and stay aligned with real-world engineering needs:

  • Context-aware model orchestration: We analyze which models, tools, and retrieval methods are best suited for specific tasks, from generating queries to correlating signals to assisting in root cause analysis. This orchestration helps AI make smarter decisions about how to help you, when to dig deeper, and when to surface something important.
  • Continuous evaluation and improvement cycles: Every release incorporates lessons from real user interactions, edge cases, and emerging patterns in observability. We refine prompts, adjust reasoning strategies, and expand the system’s understanding of how telemetry evolves. This ongoing iteration ensures that AI stays aligned with what teams need today — not what they needed a year ago.
  • Deep integration across telemetry signals: Grafana Assistant and Assistant Investigations aren’t standalone experiences; they’re woven throughout dashboards, queries, metrics, logs, traces, profiles, alerts, and more. These integrations mean that AI can operate with richer context and support workflows that span multiple components of your stack.
  • Customization and extensibility through open interfaces: We provide ways for teams to shape AI to their environment, whether that’s connecting internal systems through custom MCP servers, enriching AI with domain-specific rules or metadata, creating playbooks to guide investigations, or integrating proprietary knowledge sources that guide reasoning and responses. This allows organizations to tailor Grafana’s AI to their internal context while still benefiting from the underlying models, orchestration, and observability-aware logic we maintain.

We know that today, many organizations wrestle with the question of where to use vendor-provided AI features and where to build their own agentic systems that integrate deeply with internal tools and data. We don’t see those as mutually exclusive. 

Grafana’s AI features are designed to accelerate common observability workflows (alert summaries, query generation, root-cause analysis) without requiring teams to reinvent the wheel. But for organizations that want to go deeper, with internal knowledge graphs or custom LLM agents, Grafana’s open APIs, data model, and plugin ecosystem make that possible.

Our philosophy has always been to offer an open and composable solution. That means we expect customers to combine Grafana with their own AI innovation—not be locked into a single black-box solution.

The beauty of the Grafana ecosystem: you’re never stuck in one lane

Whether your team is early in your observability journey or operating at massive scale, the question isn’t simply build or buy anymore. It’s: What lane gets us to outcomes fastest without closing future doors?

The real strength of the Grafana ecosystem is this:

  • You can change lanes at any time: You’re never locked into the wrong decision.
  • Start open: Build where you need to. Buy for acceleration.
  • All three lanes can share the same ecosystem: A connected highway where you can shift lanes without losing speed.

Whether you’re leaning on OSS, adopting Grafana Cloud, or building specialized internal systems, Grafana gives you a common foundation that prevents your observability strategy from fragmenting over time:

  • Teams that start with OSS can move into Grafana Cloud without rethinking their data model.
  • Teams that adopt Grafana’s AI can still plug in internal agents, rules, and knowledge via open APIs.
  • Teams that build custom systems can integrate with open standards Grafana supports to avoid building yet another silo.

No matter how you approach observability and AI, the ecosystem holds together—open, composable, and built to evolve with you. And our commitment is straightforward: we will continue evolving Grafana’s AI to make it as helpful, reliable, and effective as it can be for every team that relies on us.

  • Agentic AI for Observability: Grafana Assistant & Investigations

工程中的一些问题永远不会消失。这是每个团队最终都会面临的问题：

我们是卷起袖子自己构建工具，还是购买为我们构建的工具？

这是一个能够加快团队速度或阻碍团队发展的选择。

随着人工智能驱动的可观察性的兴起，这种熟悉的软件困境随着更高的风险和更快发展的技术而重新出现。领导者需要决定：

  • 我们是否应该投入时间自己构建人工智能能力？ 
  • 或者我们是否应该采用与遥测堆栈集成、维护和发展的端到端人工智能体验？

在 Grafana Labs，我们看到组织每天都在努力做出这些决策。我们经常听到一个误解，认为选择是二元的：构建还是购买。但 Grafana 社区早就知道还有第三条道路：利用开源作为构建和购买之间的空间。

了解您的选择

事实上，现实更接近于接近路口的三车道高速公路。每个通道提供不同的速度、控制级别和维护承诺，并且每个通道都会导致不同的结果。

让我们分解一下。

通道 1. 构建：最大控制、最大开销

一些组织决定完全在内部构建人工智能驱动的可观察性功能。这意味着：

  • 设计 LLM 和代理编排
  • 连接自然语言提示以跨遥测信号生成查询
  • 构建上下文嵌入管道
  • 管理模型漂移和提示库
  • 创建接口、RBAC 和工作流程
  • 支持多信号关联逻辑
  • 维护、扩展和扩展随着时间的推移升级系统

如果提供由人工智能支持的可观察性体验是您的核心优势（例如，如果您自己就是可观察性供应商），那么在这个级别上进行投资可能是有意义的。

但对于绝大多数团队来说，构建意味着在看到有意义的结果之前投入数月的跨职能工作，并在很长一段时间后进行持续投资。 行业研究一再表明，内部构建人工智能系统涉及的不仅仅是集成模型，当团队构建管道、运营基础设施并确保系统可靠性以及正在进行的核心产品工作时，通常会减慢上市时间。 

泳道2.开源：灵活且强大，但不是完整的AI解决方案

开源是Grafana传统的闪光点。团队可以：

  • 采用Grafana、Prometheus、洛基、速度、热像仪、Alloy，以及其他OSS组件
  • 符合OpenTelemetry和其他开放标准
  • 通过Grafana MCP 服务器
  • 在 OSS 原语之上构建自动化
  • 避免锁定并保持基础设施透明

这个“中间通道”是许多组织在开始探索人工智能驱动的可观察性时使用的入口。开源为您提供了坚实的基础，无需您从头开始——经过验证的用于收集、存储和查询遥测数据的工具，基于开放标准构建，并由大型活跃社区塑造。这种灵活性允许团队随着系统和需求的发展来试验、扩展和调整他们的可观察性工作流程。

但是开源可观察性是一个基础，而不是一个完整的人工智能解决方案。将该基础转变为可用的人工智能驱动的体验需要开源无法提供开箱即用的系统。

团队必须设计和操作自己的方法来进行代理编排、提示设计和版本控制、模型选择和调整以及对人工智能行为的持续评估。他们负责决定如何组装上下文、如何验证响应、如何处理故障以及如何随着数据、使用模式和期望的发展而不断完善系统。这种额外的责任是开源灵活性的另一面。

对于某些组织来说，这种权衡不仅可以接受，而且是必需的。开源提供了托管服务难以匹敌的一定程度的控制和透明度。团队决定数据所在的位置、数据如何流经系统以及允许哪些组件访问数据。高度监管的环境、隔离网络或具有严格内部合规性要求的组织的限制可能使开源成为唯一可行的途径。

对于没有这些限制的组织，灵活性和持续所有权之间的紧张关系导致许多团队考虑采用托管方法。

第 3 条路线。购买：开箱即用的智能，根据您的情况进行调整

今天询问任何工程团队，您都会听到同样的事情：工作量很少减少，但期望总是在增长。 

当你精疲力尽时，每一个小时都很重要。在这种环境下，您必须保护团队的时间和注意力。直接支持您的核心能力的工作值得您关注，而对于其他一切，依靠减轻负担的解决方案是有意义的。

<这就是许多组织寻求托管解决方案的原因：不是因为他们无法自己构建某些东西，而是因为这根本不能充分利用团队的时间。当您的工程师可以卸下对您的业务不重要的事情时，他们就可以将更多的时间花在能够推动业务发展的工作上。

构建可观察性工具（尤其是人工智能驱动的可观察性）是我们的核心能力。

在 Grafana Labs，我们花了十多年的时间来帮助团队理解、探索和操作他们的系统。这种经验不仅塑造了我们的平台，而且塑造了我们的平台。它推动我们致力于构建真正有用的人工智能，以增强工程师的日常工作方式。我们的目标不是添加更多仪表板或更多噪音，而是减少辛苦工作、简化工作流程并帮助人们更快地做出更好的决策。

这就是我们在 Grafana Cloud 中构建人工智能辅助的两大支柱的原因：

  1. Grafana Assistant 是您整个 Grafana 生态系统的副驾驶。它可以帮助您通过自然语言交互应对复杂性，协助查询生成和优化，指导您解决遥测中的缺陷，并让任何人（不仅仅是初学者用户或可观察性专家）更轻松地快速获得答案。
  2. 助理调查就像一个专家团队与您一起工作。它不断汇集相关信号、揭示见解、识别可能的原因，并在整个问题中与您协作 — 主动帮助您了解正在发生的情况，而无需您亲自追踪每个细节。

这两种功能的构建都有着深刻的意图：让操作复杂的系统变得更容易、更快、更轻松。在幕后，我们投入了大量工作，使 Grafana Assistant 和 Assistant Investigations 对工程团队真正有用。可观察性人工智能不是一次性构建的东西，而是不断完善的东西。 

我们构建了多个基础层，决定了这些人工智能功能如何学习、适应并与现实世界的工程需求保持一致：

  • 上下文感知模型编排：我们分析哪些模型、工具和检索方法最适合特定任务，从生成查询到关联信号，再到协助根本原因分析。这种编排可以帮助 AI 做出更明智的决策，决定如何帮助您、何时深入挖掘以及何时揭示重要内容。
  • 持续评估和改进周期：每个版本都包含来自真实用户交互、边缘案例和可观察性新兴模式的经验教训。是精细提示，调整推理策略，并扩展系统对遥测如何演变的理解。这种持续的迭代确保人工智能与团队今天的需求保持一致，而不是一年前的需求。
  • 跨遥测信号的深度集成：Grafana Assistant 和 Assistant Investigations 不是独立的体验；而是一种独立的体验。它们交织在仪表板、查询、指标、日志、跟踪、配置文件、警报等中。这些集成意味着 AI 可以在更丰富的上下文中运行，并支持跨越堆栈多个组件的工作流程。
  • 通过开放接口实现自定义和可扩展性：我们为团队提供将 AI 塑造到其环境的方法，无论是通过自定义 MCP 服务器连接内部系统，还是通过特定领域丰富 AI 规则或元数据，创建剧本来指导调查，或整合指导推理和响应的专有知识源。这使得组织能够根据其内部环境定制 Grafana 的 AI，同时仍然受益于我们维护的底层模型、编排和可观察性感知逻辑。

我们知道，如今，许多组织都在努力解决以下问题：在哪里使用供应商提供的 AI 功能以及在哪里构建自己的与内部工具和数据深度集成的代理系统。 我们并不认为这些是相互排斥的。 

Grafana 的 AI 功能旨在加速常见的可观察性工作流程（警报摘要、查询生成、根本原因分析），而不需要团队重新发明轮子。但对于想要深入了解内部知识图或自定义 LLM 代理的组织来说，Grafana 的开放 API、数据模型和插件生态系统使这成为可能。

我们的理念始终是提供开放且可组合的解决方案。这意味着我们希望客户将 Grafana 与他们自己的人工智能创新相结合，而不是被锁定在单一的黑盒解决方案中。

Grafana 生态系统的美妙之处：您永远不会陷入困境

无论您的团队处于可观察性旅程的早期还是大规模运营，问题不再是简单的构建或购买。它是：什么途径可以让我们在不关闭未来大门的情况下最快地取得成果？

Grafana 生态系统的真正优势在于：

  • 您可以随时改变途径：您永远不会陷入错误的决定。
  • 开始开放：在您需要的地方构建。购买是为了加速。
  • 所有三个车道都可以共享同一个生态系统：互联的高速公路，您可以在不失去速度的情况下变换车道d.

无论您是依靠 OSS、采用 Grafana Cloud 还是构建专门的内部系统，Grafana 都为您提供了一个共同的基础，防止您的可观测性策略随着时间的推移而变得支离破碎：

  • 从 OSS 开始的团队可以迁移到 Grafana Cloud，而无需重新考虑其数据模型。
  • 采用 Grafana 人工智能的团队仍然可以插入内部代理、规则和知识通过开放 API。
  • 构建自定义系统的团队可以与 Grafana 支持的开放标准集成，以避免构建另一个孤岛。

无论您如何实现可观察性和人工智能，生态系统都会团结在一起——开放、可组合，并与您一起发展。我们的承诺很简单：我们将继续发展 Grafana 的 AI，使其为每个依赖我们的团队提供尽可能有用、可靠和高效的服务。
